Article.ArticleTitle: Construction of self-sustaining dual regeneration system through co-immobilized multienzyme and cofactor for sustainable 13-hydroxyoctadecadienoic acid synthesis.

Abstract.AbstractText: The conventional biosynthesis of 13-hydroxyoctadecadienoic acid (13-HODE) is a multi-step process, requiring to perform the enzymatic generation of 13-hydroperoxyoctadecadienoic acid (13-HPODE) and its subsequent reduction using traditional reductants in separate reaction pots. To address this challenge, we constructed a self-sustaining glutathione (GSH)-nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide phosphate (NADPH) dual regeneration system. It integrated a hydrolysis-lipoxidation-reduction cascade in one-pot, thereby supplying sustainable reducing power for the efficient synthesis of 13-HODE. We developed a co-immobilization strategy for multienzyme (lipase, lipoxygenase, glutathione reductase, glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase) together with the NADPH cofactor within a chitosan-tannic acid-Fe3+ network. This integrated one-pot system enabled sustainable 13-HODE synthesis, achieving a yield of 760.0 ± 13.3 mmol HODE/mol linoleic acid from linoleic acid and 433.37 ± 7.09 mmol HODE/mol linoleic acid from soybean oil. The co-immobilized system showed a yield approximately 2-fold higher than that of the free system, while retaining 60.37 ± 0.80 % of its initial yield over 6 cycles. This work establishes a GSH-NADPH dual-regeneration system integrated into a hydrolysis-lipoxidation-reduction cascade and provides an approach for hydroperoxide reduction and GSH-dependent bioprocessing. This co-immobilization strategy presents a sustainable and efficient platform for the production of valuable fatty acid derivatives.
